notes: Edit parameters here and push to GitHub — no Python changes needed. Weights must sum to 1.0 per species. prime_months/peak_months use 1-12. sst_sweet_spot_f is the ideal sub-range within sst_range_f. chl_required=true enables a hard disqualification gate for cells below 60% of the chl floor. sst_score_min sets a minimum SST fit score below which cells are disqualified regardless of break/CHL.

yellowfin.structure_notes: Warm-water eddies detached from Gulf Stream; The Point shelf structure; color changes; current seams. Satellite SST essential night before.
yellowfin.seasonal_context: April = June peak off OBX; spring is best. Summer lull typical. Fish the warm side of the break, warm-core eddies spinning off the Stream are the primary aggregators. The most important thing isn't a specific temperature, it's finding sharp thermal breaks, a 2–5°F change over a short horizontal distance concentrates bait and predators. The Gulf Stream influence is everything here; you're looking for that warm blue water to push close to the canyons. Most action happens in 200 – 1000+ fathoms, but the key structure is: Canyon edges and walls — The Norfolk, Washington, Baltimore, and Hatteras canyons are all within reach. The shelf break (roughly 100 fm) is where warm Gulf Stream water meets the cooler shelf water, creating upwelling and bait concentrations. The 200-fathom shelf break is particularly significant — this is where you start transitioning into blue water and where pelagics patrol. Rips and color changes along canyon walls and the stream edge are high-percentage spots. Temperature breaks on the surface that align with bottom structure below are jackpot zones. You want deep cobalt/royal blue water — high clarity, minimal particulate. Avoid green or dirty brown water. The ideal scenario is finding a clean blue-to-green color change (a rip) that coincides with a temperature break. YFT will stack right on that edge. Avoid water with excessive Sargassum mats unless there's bait underneath — sometimes that's productive, sometimes it just kills trolling speed. You want to be near elevated chlorophyll, but not in it. High-chlorophyll water (greener on satellite imagery) holds bait — flying fish, squid, mahi — but yellowfin themselves prefer the clean side of the chlorophyll gradient. Look for chlorophyll fronts where values drop from ~0.3–0.5 mg/m³ (productive green water) to <0.1 mg/m³ (blue). YFT cruise that edge picking off bait that aggregates there.

bluefin.structure_notes: Giants stage where cold Labrador current meets the warm Gulf Stream wall. Large bait schools (menhaden, mackerel) are the magnet. Canyon edges and cold-core eddies.
bluefin.seasonal_context: Dec-Mar peak; Jan-Feb best for giants. Colder, rougher conditions keep bluefin around longer. Fish the cold inshore side of the Gulf Stream wall. Trolling and jigging both effective.

mahi.structure_notes: Floating weedlines, Sargassum mats, debris, FADs. Temperature breaks with associated color change (blue-green transition). Chlorophyll front matters as much as SST. Zones with near-zero CHL are disqualified — Mahi are not found in barren blue water. SST must be a good fit — cloud-edge false breaks in wrong-temperature water are filtered by sst_score_min.
mahi.seasonal_context: Schools are often schoolie-sized (5–15 lbs) this early in the season but bigger bulls and cows show up fast. May-Jul best for gaffers. Smaller class fish outside that window. They ride the warm side of color changes and travel along the break. Mahi actually tolerate moderate chlorophyll water better, they follow Sargassum which concentrates in convergence zones with elevated chlorophyll. Look for weed lines that form along chlorophyll fronts. Intersections of SST + chlorophyll will help identify where those Sargassum lines are likely accumulating, they tend to pile up where two water masses meet.

wahoo.structure_notes: Rock Pile (180 ft), 200 Rocks, pinnacles south of Hatteras. Floating logs, weedlines, ledges. High-speed trolling wire-rigged lures. Full/new moon tides increase bite.
wahoo.seasonal_context: Jul-Oct peak; fall gigs around Oct. Some Dec-Jan action in warm years. Found on the inshore/upcurrent (cold) side of rock structure and temp breaks — the cold side holds bait and wahoo stack behind the break.

kingfish.structure_notes: Wrecks, reefs, nearshore shoals, inlet ledges. Schools follow bait pods (menhaden, cigar minnows). Live bait most effective for big smokers. Spanish mackerel presence signals kings are near.
kingfish.seasonal_context: Mar-Nov; spring and fall migration peaks. Big smokers peak in Oct. Narrow 68-73 F SST window — one of the most temp-specific species. Follow bait more than break side. In summer heat, cooler side of a break can produce.

white_marlin.structure_notes: Warm, clear blue water with bait concentration (squid, flying fish). Stable eddy seams — NOT fast-running current rips. Trolling ballyhoo and small skirted lures. Satellite imaging essential to locate eddy pockets.
white_marlin.seasonal_context: Late Jul-Sep; classic peak is second week of August at Oregon Inlet. North of Cape Hatteras best where stable eddies form more frequently. Whites prefer slow-turning warm eddies shoreward of the Stream. Clarity and current stability matter as much as temperature.

blue_marlin.structure_notes: Deep offshore Gulf Stream and warm-core eddies. Most temp-tolerant billfish. Largest fish prefer the cooler edge of the 74-82 F band. Trolling large skirted lures and rigged baits at speed.
blue_marlin.seasonal_context: Jun-Sep; peak Jul-Aug. Broadest SST tolerance of all target species (71-88 F). Deep water specialist: 100fm+ (600ft+). Largest fish prefer cooler edge of the action band. Follow the Gulf Stream edge and warm eddies.
